Integration of renewable energy sources in tandem with electrolysis: A technology review for green hydrogen production - ScienceDirect Integration of renewable energy sources in tandem with electrolysis: A technology review for green hydrogen production Despite its potential, integrating renewable energy with electrolysis to produce green hydrogen faces significant technological and economic challenges, particularly in achieving high efficiency and cost-effectiveness at scale. While some focus on integrated green hydrogen production with a single energy source, others emphasise hybrid renewable energy systems , , , , , , , , , ], Vidas and Castro [12] focused on integrated hydrogen value chain and water splitting by green electrolysis. Integration of renewable energy sources in tandem with electrolysis: A technology review for green hydrogen production
======================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Somtochukwu Godfrey Nnabuife a, Abdulhammed K.Hamzat b, James Whidborne c, Boyu Kuang d, Karl W.Jenkins d

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2024.06.342](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2024.06.342 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S0360319924025825&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •Green hydrogen production technologies are comprehensively reviewed. 
*   •Details challenges and solutions in renewable-hydrogen integration. 
*   •Holistic consideration of interconnected variables affecting levelized hydrogen costs. 
*   •Dynamic operation of PEM electrolyser enhances the electricity grid flexibility. 
*   •Grey areas that need to be explored for technological advancement are emphasised. 

Abstract
--------

The global shift toward sustainable energy solutions emphasises the urgent need to harness renewable sources for green [hydrogen production](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/hydrogen-production "Learn more about hydrogen production from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), presenting a critical opportunity in the transition to a low-carbon economy. Despite its potential, integrating renewable energy with electrolysis to produce [green hydrogen](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/green-hydrogen "Learn more about green hydrogen from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") faces significant technological and economic challenges, particularly in achieving high efficiency and cost-effectiveness at scale. This review systematically examines the latest advancements in electrolysis technologies—alkaline, [proton exchange membrane](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/proton-exchange-membrane "Learn more about proton exchange membrane from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[electrolysis cell](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/electrolysis-cell "Learn more about electrolysis cell from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (PEMEC), and solid oxide—and explores innovative grid integration and [energy storage solutions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/energy-storage-solution "Learn more about energy storage solutions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that enhance the viability of green hydrogen. The study reveals enhanced performance metrics in electrolysis processes and identifies critical factors that influence the operational efficiency and sustainability of green [hydrogen production](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/hydrogen-production "Learn more about hydrogen production from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Key findings demonstrate the potential for substantial reductions in the cost and energy requirements of hydrogen production by optimising [electrolyser](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/electrolyser "Learn more about electrolyser from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") design and operation. The insights from this research provide a foundational strategy for scaling up green hydrogen as a sustainable energy carrier, contributing to global efforts to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and advance toward [carbon neutrality](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/carbon-neutrality "Learn more about carbon neutrality from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The integration of these technologies could revolutionise [energy systems](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/energy-systems "Learn more about energy systems from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") worldwide, aligning with policy frameworks and market dynamics to foster broader adoption of green hydrogen.

1. Introduction
---------------

The global quest for sustainable energy solutions has become necessary to minimise [climate change](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/climate-change "Learn more about climate change from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and reduce reliance on [fossil fuels](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/fossil-fuel "Learn more about fossil fuels from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Hydrogen, as a clean energy carrier, is uniquely capable of storing and transporting renewable energy, thus playing a [pivotal role](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/pivotal-role "Learn more about pivotal role from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in the global energy transition [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib1)]. Particularly, the production of green hydrogen—generated through [water electrolysis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/water-electrolysis "Learn more about water electrolysis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") using electricity sourced from renewable energies—offers a compelling solution to reduce reliance on fossil fuels and significantly cut greenhouse gas emissions [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib1),[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib2)]. Therefore, developing and optimising efficient technologies for green [hydrogen production](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/hydrogen-production "Learn more about hydrogen production from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is crucial for achieving global carbon reduction targets and advancing the commercialisation of [clean energy technologies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/clean-energy-technology "Learn more about clean energy technologies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Integrating [renewable energy sources](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/renewable-energy-source "Learn more about renewable energy sources from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with modern technology has become a powerful path towards a cleaner and more resilient energy future in this paradigm shift. Among several technological constraints, the harmonious integration of renewable energy sources with [electrolysis technology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/electrolysis-technology "Learn more about electrolysis technology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") has received increasing attention. This study begins a detailed analysis of the evolving environment around integrating renewable energy sources, notably in the context of [green hydrogen](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/green-hydrogen "Learn more about green hydrogen from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") generation with electrolysis.

This article highlights the path to unlocking the potential of green hydrogen as the foundation of a sustainable energy ecosystem by thoroughly examining technological breakthroughs, obstacles, and possibilities. Hydrogen can serve as a significant energy carrier in the process of decarbonisation. It has the potential to be used in several areas, such as transportation, heating in buildings, industrial processes, and [power generation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/power-generation "Learn more about power generation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Several factors can either facilitate or hinder the speed at which technology is learned, and infrastructure is developed to ensure a dependable and affordable energy supply [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib3)]. This is particularly relevant for the production of green hydrogen in regions with abundant solar resources, such as the Middle East and North Africa (MENA) [[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib4),[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib5)]. From a global standpoint, green hydrogen is anticipated to be traded worldwide in neutral [energy systems](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/energy-systems "Learn more about energy systems from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") regarding greenhouse gas emissions. However, this will only be possible if a thorough assessment of the entire process chain is crucial for developing effective global policies [[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib6)]. Additionally, in the United States [[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib7)], green hydrogen is considered a viable and environmentally friendly method for attaining [carbon neutrality](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/carbon-neutrality "Learn more about carbon neutrality from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Despite its substantial benefits, the production and handling of hydrogen, particularly green hydrogen, present several critical challenges. These include the high costs of electrolysis equipment, the technical complexities associated with integrating fluctuating renewable energy sources, and the substantial energy requirements for [hydrogen compression](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/hydrogen-compression "Learn more about hydrogen compression from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), storage, and transportation. Handling hydrogen also introduces safety risks due to its highly flammable nature and low ignition energy, necessitating advanced safety protocols and systems.

Green hydrogen is produced through water electrolysis using electricity derived from renewable energy sources, offering unprecedented potential as a clean and adaptable energy source. Unlike its traditional competitors, the generation of green hydrogen emits much less greenhouse gas emissions, making it a potent weapon for combating climate change across industries. Applications range from powering automobiles and businesses to acting as a storage medium for emerging renewable energy sources. Hydrogen production technologies involve four critical stages: production, storage, transportation, and use. Serious attention and significant improvements are needed in those stages to revolutionise [hydrogen production](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/hydrogen-production "Learn more about hydrogen production from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The transportation of hydrogen from the production site to the consuming site is a crucial operation in the hydrogen manufacturing economy since it can lead to higher production costs, hence affecting its affordability. The storage of hydrogen is regarded as a pressing and challenging task as it facilitates the advancement of secure, dependable, efficient, and sufficient storage systems [[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib8)]. The proportion of hydrogen produced from sustainable sources is very low compared to fossil fuels hydrogen-driven production due to the exorbitant cost associated with water analysis, [hydrogen storage](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/hydrogen-storage "Learn more about hydrogen storage from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and huge infrastructural investment. Also, Fuel cells utilised for converting hydrogen into electricity exhibit lower efficiency compared to alternative energy conversion processes. Consequently, more significant quantities of hydrogen must be consumed to produce equivalent energy [[[9]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib9), [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib10), [[11]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib11)]. Despite these challenges, it is anticipated that the advancement in technology through integrating renewable energy sources with the production and utilisation of green energy will address the highlighted issues and opportunities associated with expanding the production, storage, and distribution infrastructure. However, realising this potential necessitates a full knowledge of the integration of renewable energy and electricity generation, which is the primary goal of this research.

Review articles on various topics have already been published on the subject of hydrogen systems of energy. While some focus on integrated green hydrogen production with a single energy source, others emphasise hybrid [renewable energy systems](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/renewable-energy-system "Learn more about renewable energy systems from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib12), [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib13), [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib14), [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib15), [[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib16), [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib17), [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib18), [[19]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib19), [[20]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib20), [[21]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib21)], Vidas and Castro [[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib12)] focused on integrated hydrogen value chain and water splitting by green electrolysis. The authors fail to explore grid integration and green energy storage. Recently, Kojima et al. [[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib13)] provided information about the current fluctuating power and generation status in the water [electrolyser](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/electrolyser "Learn more about electrolyser from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") configuration. The author fails to address the impact of Hybrid renewable energy integrated green hydrogen production.

Mazloomi and Gomes [[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib22)] considered the manufacture and storage techniques in addition to the risks and safety concerns associated with the technology, hydrogen is a very viable option both as a fuel for future automobiles and as a form of energy storage in massive [power systems](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/power-engineering "Learn more about power systems from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Thema et al. [[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib23)] studied power-to-gas initiatives that create hydrogen or renewable gas to replace natural gas, including a cost-development estimate for electrolysis and [carbon dioxide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/carbon-dioxide "Learn more about carbon dioxide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[methanation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/methanation "Learn more about methanation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Dutta [[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib24)] examined [hydrogen generation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/hydrogen-generation "Learn more about hydrogen generation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and storage techniques while also paying attention to risk and security concerns. In their study of hydrogen as a potential primary energy carrier, Abe et al. [[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib25)] put particular emphasis on how hydrogen is stored in [metal hydrides](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/metal-hydride "Learn more about metal hydrides from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Gahleitner [[26](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib26)] studied power-to-gas pilot plants that utilised renewable electricity to electrolyse water to create hydrogen. Eveloy and Gebreegziabher [[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib27)] analysis of anticipated power-to-gas scenarios revealed the need for significant advancements in fields like effectiveness, affordability, and reliability if widespread adoption of these kinds of systems eventually becomes feasible. The possibilities for storing and delivering hydrogen were explored, and risk and security concerns were examined by Ref. [[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib28)]. Hanley et al. [[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib29)] reviewed the use of hydrogen in energy networks and examined potential factors and regulations that would make hydrogen more advantageous than other low-emission energy sources. They discovered that the case with the greatest likelihood is one in which hydrogen technologies are mostly used after 2030. With an emphasis on mass manufacturing, standardisation, and supportive policies, Parra et al. [[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib30)] present a techno-economic analysis of [hydrogen energy systems](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/hydrogen-energy-systems "Learn more about hydrogen energy systems from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and emphasise policies that they believe may hasten the widespread use of hydrogen technology. By providing a synopsis of actual projects, Bailera et al. [[31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib31)] evaluated the various processes utilised to transform renewable energy sources into methane in power-to-gas projects. Power-to-gas initiatives in Europe were taken into account by Ref. [[32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib32)], those who hypothesised that power-to-gas plants will be crucial for refineries in the coming years to lower the emissions associated with their products.

Badreldin et al. [[33](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib33)] critically examine various [oxidation processes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/oxidation-reaction "Learn more about oxidation processes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The analysis covers reactions that use sacrificial reagents, such as wastewater pollutants, as targets. Processes that source biomass and then partially reform it to [produce hydrogen](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/produce-hydrogen "Learn more about produce hydrogen from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and beneficial byproducts were also looked at. The analysis presented here provides an understandable overview of the existing difficulties and barriers associated with using alternate oxidation processes as a strategy to address industrial hydrogen generation demands. Important technical insights were also offered to further the use of alternate oxidation processes. The review by Chatterjee et al. [[34](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib34)] presents the current PV-PEC technological integration for green hydrogen. They start by discussing the basics of [PV](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/photovoltaics "Learn more about PV from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and water splitting techniques (photocatalysis, electrolysis, photo [electrocatalysis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/electrocatalysis "Learn more about electrocatalysis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (PEC), and electrocatalysis (EC), in addition to the reasons and how independently solar water splitting equipment evolved from [PV](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/photovoltaics "Learn more about PV from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with external electrolysers (PV-EC) to the incorporation of PV with EC (IPV-EC) and PEC (PV-PEC). The authors then go over the principal difficulties in PV-PEC incorporation and give an overview of the key developments in conception and material science for high solar-to-hydrogen (STH) efficiency and extended device lifetimes. Cu-oxide is emphasised as a developing material, along with the significance of material choice and metal-oxide [semiconductor nanostructures](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/semiconductor-nanostructures "Learn more about semiconductor nanostructures from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for PV-PEC integration. Other sources of renewable energy integrated with green hydrogen were not considered. Rumayor et al. [[35](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360319924025825#bib35)] used super-green hydrogen, commonly known as photo-hydrogen, to demonstrate a potential performance scenario that may be a viable alternative for electro-hydrogen. The research has produced future possibilities by cal

--- SOURCE 2: Hydrogen Production: Electrolysis ---
URL: https://www.energy.gov/eere/fuelcells/hydrogen-production-electrolysis

SUMMARY:
Solid oxide electrolyzers, which use a solid ceramic material as the electrolyte that selectively conducts negatively charged oxygen ions (O 2-) at elevated temperatures, generate hydrogen in a slightly different way. The solid oxide electrolyzers can effectively use heat available at these elevated temperatures (from various sources, including nuclear energy) to decrease the amount of electrical energy needed to produce hydrogen from water. Hydrogen production via electrolysis may offer opportunities for synergy with dynamic and intermittent power generation, which is characteristic of some renewable energy technologies. Electricity generation using renewable or nuclear energy technologies, either separate from the grid, or as a growing portion of the grid mix, is a possible option to overcome these limitations for hydrogen production via electrolysis.

Hydrogen Production: Electrolysis
=================================

Electrolysis is a promising option for carbon-free [hydrogen production](https://www.energy.gov/eere/fuelcells/hydrogen-production) from renewable and nuclear resources. Electrolysis is the process of using electricity to split water into hydrogen and oxygen. This reaction takes place in a unit called an electrolyzer. Electrolyzers can range in size from small, appliance-size equipment that is well-suited for small-scale distributed hydrogen production to large-scale, central production facilities that could be tied directly to renewable or other non-greenhouse-gas-emitting forms of electricity production.

![Image 5: Schematic of a polymer electrolyte membrane electrolyzer](https://www.energy.gov/sites/default/files/styles/full_article_width/public/2021-07/hfto-electrolyzer-illustration.png?itok=s8WlBKKg)

How Does it Work?
-----------------

Like fuel cells, electrolyzers consist of an anode and a cathode separated by an electrolyte. Different electrolyzers function in different ways, mainly due to the different type of electrolyte material involved and the ionic species it conducts.

### Polymer Electrolyte Membrane Electrolyzers

In a polymer electrolyte membrane (PEM) electrolyzer, the electrolyte is a solid specialty plastic material.

*   Water reacts at the anode to form oxygen and positively charged hydrogen ions (protons).
*   The electrons flow through an external circuit and the hydrogen ions selectively move across the PEM to the cathode.
*   At the cathode, hydrogen ions combine with electrons from the external circuit to form hydrogen gas. Anode Reaction: 2H 2 O → O 2 + 4H+ + 4e- Cathode Reaction: 4H+ + 4e- → 2H 2

### Alkaline Electrolyzers

Alkaline electrolyzers operate via transport of hydroxide ions (OH-) through the electrolyte from the cathode to the anode with hydrogen being generated on the cathode side. Electrolyzers using a liquid alkaline solution of sodium or potassium hydroxide as the electrolyte have been commercially available for many years. Newer approaches using solid alkaline exchange membranes (AEM) as the electrolyte are showing promise on the lab scale.

### Solid Oxide Electrolyzers

Solid oxide electrolyzers, which use a solid ceramic material as the electrolyte that selectively conducts negatively charged oxygen ions (O 2-) at elevated temperatures, generate hydrogen in a slightly different way.

*   Steam at the cathode combines with electrons from the external circuit to form hydrogen gas and negatively charged oxygen ions.
*   The oxygen ions pass through the solid ceramic membrane and react at the anode to form oxygen gas and generate electrons for the external circuit.

Solid oxide electrolyzers must operate at temperatures high enough for the solid oxide membranes to function properly (about 700°–800°C, compared to PEM electrolyzers, which operate at 70°–90°C, and commercial alkaline electrolyzers, which typically operate at less than 100°C). Advanced lab-scale solid oxide electrolyzers based on proton-conducting ceramic electrolytes are showing promise for lowering the operating temperature to 500°–600°C. The solid oxide electrolyzers can effectively use heat available at these elevated temperatures (from various sources, including nuclear energy) to decrease the amount of electrical energy needed to produce hydrogen from water.

Why Is This Pathway Being Considered?
-------------------------------------

Electrolysis is a leading hydrogen production pathway to achieve the [Hydrogen Energy Earthshot](https://www.energy.gov/eere/fuelcells/hydrogen-shot) goal of reducing the cost of clean hydrogen by 80% to $1 per 1 kilogram in 1 decade ("1 1 1"). Hydrogen produced via electrolysis can result in zero greenhouse gas emissions, depending on the source of the electricity used. The source of the required electricity—including its cost and efficiency, as well as emissions resulting from electricity generation—must be considered when evaluating the benefits and economic viability of hydrogen production via electrolysis. In many regions of the country, today's power grid is not ideal for providing the electricity required for electrolysis because of the greenhouse gases released and the amount of fuel required due to the low efficiency of the electricity generation process. Hydrogen production via electrolysis is being pursued for renewable (wind, solar, hydro, geothermal) and nuclear energy options. These hydrogen production pathways result in virtually zero greenhouse gas and criteria pollutant emissions; however, the production cost needs to be decreased significantly to be competitive with more mature carbon-based pathways such as natural gas reforming.

**Potential for synergy with renewable energy power generation**

 Hydrogen production via electrolysis may offer opportunities for synergy with dynamic and intermittent power generation, which is characteristic of some renewable energy technologies. For example, though the cost of wind power has continued to drop, the inherent variability of wind is an impediment to the effective use of wind power. Hydrogen fuel and electric power generation could be integrated at a wind farm, allowing flexibility to shift production to best match resource availability with system operational needs and market factors. Also, in times of excess electricity production from wind farms, instead of curtailing the electricity as is commonly done, it is possible to use this excess electricity to produce hydrogen through electrolysis.

**It is important to note...**

*   Today's grid electricity is not the ideal source of electricity for electrolysis because most of the electricity is generated using technologies that result in greenhouse gas emissions and are energy intensive. Electricity generation using renewable or nuclear energy technologies, either separate from the grid, or as a growing portion of the grid mix, is a possible option to overcome these limitations for hydrogen production via electrolysis.
*   The U.S. Department of Energy and others continue efforts to bring down the cost of renewable-based electricity production and develop more efficient fossil-fuel-based electricity production with carbon capture, utilization, and storage. Wind-based electricity production, for example, is growing rapidly in the United States and globally.

Research Focuses On Overcoming Challenges
-----------------------------------------

*   Meeting the Hydrogen Shot clean hydrogen cost target of $1/kg H 2 by 2030 (and interim target of $2/kg H 2 by 2025) through improved understanding of performance, cost, and durability trade-offs of electrolyzer systems under predicted future dynamic operating modes using CO 2-free electricity.
*   Reducing the capital cost of the electrolyzer unit and the balance of the system.
*   Improving energy efficiency for converting electricity to hydrogen over a wide range of operating conditions.
*   Increasing understanding of electrolyzer cell and stack degradation processes and developing mitigation strategies to increase operational life.

# Your guide to electrolysis: the tech behind the green hydrogen revolution

## Sundus Cordelia Ramli

![Your guide to electrolysis: the tech behind the green hydrogen revolution featured image](https://www.topsoe.com/hubfs/Your%20guide%20to%20electrolysis%20the%20tech%20behind%20the%20green%20hydrogen%20revolution.png)

## Key takeaways

In an era marked by growing environmental concerns and the urgent need to transition towards cleaner and more sustainable energy sources, electrolysis has emerged as a transformative technology with the potential to revolutionize the energy landscape. It is the technology that facilitates the creation of [green hydrogen](/processes/green-hydrogen).  
  
Electrolysis is a process that harnesses electrical energy to split water molecules into hydrogen and oxygen gases. When the process is powered by renewable energy, it can be used to create green hydrogen. That green hydrogen can then, in turn, be used as a clean energy carrier.  
  
The potential of green hydrogen to decarbonize hard-to-abate industries — shipping, aviation, steel, cement and petrochemical production — is vast. In fact, these industries account for roughly 30% of all greenhouse gas emissions.  
  
Here’s everything you need to know about the technology responsible for the green hydrogen revolution.

### Electrolysis will provide these sectors with green hydrogen

Transportation  
Green hydrogen can be used in fuel cell vehicles, providing a zero-emission alternative to traditional internal combustion engines. Green ammonia and e-methanol, which are derivatives of green hydrogen, are currently being explored as key solutions in decarbonizing the world's industrial-scale transportation industries. This is particularly relevant in the global shipping industry, where there are projects set to be tested and developed as early as 2024.  
  
Industry  
Steel, cement and chemical production are among the industries with the highest level of emissions and, unfortunately, have the most difficulty in decarbonizing. This is partly due to the fact that many of the manufacturing processes across these industries require a large amount of energy to produce the high temperature heat needed for production. Luckily, these energy intensive processes can use green hydrogen as a substitute, opening up the possibility to produce products such as ‘green steel’ — where green hydrogen is used to generate heat and takes the place of coal and natural gas in facilitating chemical processes.  
  
Energy storage  
Green chemicals can also serve as energy storage mediums, allowing excess renewable energy from wind and solar to be stored and later converted back to electricity when needed. This helps stabilize the grid and supports the integration of intermittent renewable sources.

### 3 types of electrolysis: what you need to know

Electrolysis is primarily achieved through three types of industrial technologies: high-temperature Solid-Oxide Electrolysis Cell ([SOEC](/soec)); low-temperature alkaline electrolysis; and low-temperature polymer electrolyte membrane (PEM) electrolysis. With alkaline and PEM electrolysis, water is supplied as a liquid, whereas SOEC electrolysis uses steam due to its high temperatures.  
  
PEM electrolysis  
PEM electrolysis uses a solid polymer electrolyte membrane to separate the hydrogen and oxygen gases. This membrane allows for high proton conductivity, a key process in the creation of green hydrogen, while preventing the mixing of gases. It operates at relatively low temperatures, between 50-80°C, and is known for its rapid response time.  
  
PEM electrolysis systems are compact, modular and well-suited for intermittent renewable energy sources like wind and solar. They can quickly adjust their output to match fluctuations in energy supply.  
  
PEM electrolysis systems tend to be more expensive due to the cost of the membrane material. The market for PEM electrolysis is well established, particularly in applications requiring high purity hydrogen, such as fuel cell vehicles for pipelines. The market is expected to grow as renewable energy adoption expands.  
  
SOEC electrolysis  
SOEC electrolysis employs a solid oxide ceramic electrolyte that operates at high temperature, typically around 675°C to 825°C. At these temperatures, the water electrolysis reaction is easier to drive, which in turn results in a lower power consumption per unit of hydrogen produced.  
  
SOEC offers higher efficiency than PEM and alkaline electrolysis, and can make use of waste heat from industrial processes or concentrated solar power. It is well-suited to large-scale hydrogen production with uses including steel, ammonia and chemicals production and refining.  
  
The International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA) estimate that SOEC electrolyzers are between 10-26% more efficient (by kWh per kg of hydrogen produced) than alkaline and PEM technologies.  
  
SOEC electrolysis is a relatively nascent technology compared to PEM and alkaline for many applications. However, the technology holds promise for industrial and energy storage applications, especially where high-temperature heat sources and high heat-waste emissions are available.  
  
Alkaline electrolysis  
Alkaline electrolysis uses an alkaline electrolyte solution, usually potassium hydroxide, to facilitate the ion exchange process that makes hydrogen. It operates at moderate temperatures and has been used for decades in industrial applications.  
  
Alkaline electrolyzers are cost-effective and have a long history of commercial use. However, Alkaline electrolysis systems are less efficient and slower to respond to load changes compared to PEM electrolysis.  
  
Alkaline electrolysis remains competitive in certain industrial sectors but may face challenges in terms of efficiency and adaptability to renewable energy integration.

### The opportunity of electrolysis

A green energy economy will not be enabled through any one single idea or technology. However, electrolyzers will be instrumental in creating green transitions for some of the world’s most carbon-intensive industries.  
  
Green hydrogen is the bridge between a wind turbine, or a solar panel, to fuels we use in our everyday lives, in planes, in cars or in ships — and electrolysis makes that happen.  
  
The emerging green hydrogen economy presents substantial economic prospects, fostering job creation and driving innovation and investment in clean energy technologies, and bolsters energy security.  
  
Continued research, innovation and investment in electrolysis is essential — it is a major opportunity that will reap dividends for those who act fast.

enriched_points.expanded_content: PEM electrolysis achieves efficiency rates of up to 91%, making it ideal for sustainable hydrogen production, especially when powered by renewable energy.
PEM systems can quickly adjust their hydrogen output, making them well-suited for intermittent renewable sources like solar and wind, enhancing grid stability.
PEM electrolysis can seamlessly integrate with solar and wind power, allowing excess energy to convert into hydrogen, thereby reducing waste and enhancing overall energy efficiency.
PEM technology is currently utilized in fuel cell vehicles, industrial processes, and energy storage solutions, highlighting its versatility and effectiveness in the hydrogen economy.
